request.question: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?

title: The Brothers Cazimero
Mākaha Sons
Theryl DeClouet
Israel Kamakawiwoʻole
Elliot James
Jean Sebastien Gerard
McGruff (rapper)
Truth in Music Advertising
Timothy James Webb
Sons of Hawaii
sentences: The Brothers Cazimero was a Hawaiian musical duo made up of Robert Cazimero on bass and Roland Cazimero on twelve string guitar.
Robert also played piano as a solo musician.
The Cazimeros got their start during the Hawaiian Renaissance with ukulele and slack-key guitarist Peter Moon's band, The Sunday Manoa, on their first recording, "Guava Jam".
Since that time, The Brothers Cazimero have released at least 36 recordings and three DVDs.
For three decades, the group performed at the annual Lei Day Concert.
They made their Carnegie Hall debut in 1989.
The Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau are a Hawaiian musical group formed in 1976 on the island of Oahu by Jerome Koko, Louis "Moon" Kauakahi, Skippy Kamakawiwo'ole, Israel Kamakawiwoʻole, and Sam Gray.
The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.
The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.
After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.
Theryl DeClouet (born September 17, 1951, in Hollygrove, New Orleans, Louisiana), also known as House Man, is a soul/R&B singer, best known as the former lead vocalist for the musical group Galactic.
He appeared on the band's first four studio albums as well as a and a compilation before health concerns forced his departure from the band and its heavy tour schedule.
His latest independent solo release, "The Truth Iz Out", features support from longtime friend Ivan Neville and guitarist June Yamagishi of Papa Grows Funk.
He also appeared on Charlie Hunter Quintet's 2001 album, "Songs from the Analog Playground", Hunter's first to feature vocals.
DeClouet sang on versions of Earth, Wind & Fire's "Mighty Mighty" and the Willie Dixon standard "Spoonful."

Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.
Elliot James Mulhern (born September 29, 1989) known professionally as Elliot James, is a British singer songwriter, producer, and musician also known as the drummer of rock band Hey Monday.
He owns a music publishing and production company Fever Mint.
He left Hey Monday fall of 2009 and toured with various acts including The Scene Aesthetic.
James is the owner of record label, Blossöm Records.
He produced three records on Jallal's album "Off The Radar" with Chad Hugo, Ne-Yo, BJ the Chicago Kid, and Jan Fairchild.
Jean-Sébastien Gerard, born 1970, is a musician also known as Jess from Overlanders, who composed chiptunes for the Atari ST demoscene in the 90s.
Herbert Brown, better known by his stage name McGruff (also known as Herb McGruff) is an American Hip hop recording artist from Harlem, New York City, New York.
Brown started rapping in his early teens.
He embarked on his music career in the hip hop group Bronx Most Wanted, alongside rappers Jay Q and Tee U.B. Brown later became a member of the hip hop collective Children of the Corn.
As a member of Children of the Corn, Brown worked alongside Big L, Cam'ron, Bloodshed and Mase, all of whom would go on to have successful careers in the music industry.

After the group disbanded without releasing any material, Brown made his first appearance on Big L's debut album, "Lifestylez ov da Poor & Dangerous" (1995), on the tracks "8 Iz Enuff" and "Dangerzone".
Eventually, Brown signed a deal with popular hip hop label, Uptown Records and began recording his debut album, "Destined to Be".
In early 1998, the album's lead single "Before We Start" became a minor hit, charting on several "Billboard" charts.
In the Summer of 1998, "Destined to Be" was released but failed to sell many copies, only peaking at 169 on the "Billboard" 200, and Brown was released from his contract.
Brown would make appearances with Heavy D on his album, "Waterbed Hev" and the "Woo" soundtrack.
In 2009, Brown appeared on Mase's mixtape "I Do the Impossible".
In 2010, he was featured heavily on The Diplomats mixtape, "The D.I.P. Agenda."
In 2014, DJ Kay Slay, enlisted Brown, along with Raekwon, Fat Joe, Ghostface Killah, Sheek Louch, N.O.R.E., Lil' Fame, Prodigy and Rell, for a song titled "90s Flow".

The Truth in Music Advertising act or bill, also known as Truth in Music Performance Advertising or simply Truth in Music, is legislation, adopted into state law by most U.S. states, that aims to protect the trademark of musical recording artists.
The legislation provides that the name of a famous musical group cannot be used by a group of performers unless they include at least one member of the original group.
The intent of the legislation is to prevent unfair or deceptive trade practices, and to protect the livelihood of musicians who were in famous musical groups.
Timothy James Webb, as a musician also known as Tim J. Webb (born Warburton, Victoria, 13 February 1967), is an Australian painter and sculptor, who has been living in Munich, Germany, since 2000.
The Sons of Hawaii was a Hawaiian musical group that grew popular to mainstream audiences from the 1960s through the 1990s.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: The musician also known as "Braddah Iz"  is a member of what Hawaiian musical group?
hop1_docs: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole | Israel Kaʻanoʻi Kamakawiwoʻole (] , translation: "The Fearless Eyed"; May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997), also called "Braddah Iz" (Brother Iz), was a Native Hawaiian musician, entertainer and Hawaiian sovereignty activist.

Alone in IZ World | Alone in IZ World is an album by the Hawaiian musician Israel Kamakawiwo'ole released in 2001, 4 years after his death in 1997.  The album has charted on several of Billboard’s album charts.  These are:

Gosti iz budushchego | Gosti iz budushchego (Russian: Гости из будущего , lit.: "Guests from the Future") was a Russian dance-pop group, which existed since 1996 until 2009.  It was formed by the singer Eva Polna and musician Yuri Usachyov.  The band reached high positions in Russian music charts with several songs and albums.

Kuana Torres Kahele | Kuana Torres Kahele (born September 7, 1978) is a Hawaiian musician, vocalist, songwriter, record producer, dancer, and educator from Hilo, Hawaii.  Known primarily for his original acoustic compositions, Kahele arranges traditional Hawaiian music as well, performing and recording with instruments such as ukulele, guitar, ipu, and bass.  The majority of his lyrics are written and performed in the Hawaiian language.  After learning kane dancing and mele writing in his teens, in 1995 Kahele co-founded Nā Palapalai, a Hawaiian music group that has released seven albums.  Several have charted in the top five on the "Billboard" Top World Albums chart, and the group has won a large number of Nā Hōkū Hanohano Awards, including Group of the Year.
N Dis Life | In Dis Life is the final album of original music by Native Hawaiian singer Israel "Iz" Kamakawiwo'ole before his death, released in 1996.  It reached #3 at Top World Music Albums.

Dennis Pavao | Dennis Pavao (July 11, 1951 - January 19, 2002), was one of several Hawaiian musicians who, during the 1970s, led a Hawaiian music renaissance, reviving Hawaiian music, especially "ka leo ki'eki'e," or Hawaiian falsetto singing.  Along with his cousins, Ledward and Nedward Kaʻ apana, Pavao started the group Hui ʻ Ohana.  Hui ʻ Ohana became the premier falsetto group in Hawaiʻ i.  After the breakup of Hui ʻ Ohana, Dennis Pavao moved on to pursue a solo career.

Cold Case Files (album) | Cold Case Files is a compilation album released by rap group, Onyx.  It was released on August 19, 2008 through the Iceman Music Group and contained unreleased tracks from the group's first three albums, Bacdafucup, All We Got Iz Us and Shut 'Em Down.  Deceased member, Big DS and affiliate, X-1 also appear on the album.

- Israel Kamakawiwoʻole — [1]: "also called \"Braddah Iz\" (Brother Iz)"
- Israel Kamakawiwoʻole — [1]: "May 20, 1959 – June 26, 1997"
- Alone in IZ World — [2]: "Alone in IZ World is an album"
- Israel Kamakawiwo'ole (variant) — [2]: "by the Hawaiian musician Israel Kamakawiwo'ole"
- In Dis Life — [5]: "In Dis Life is the final album"
- Israel "Iz" Kamakawiwo'ole (variant) — [5]: "by Native Hawaiian singer Israel \"Iz\" Kamakawiwo'ole"
- Kuana Torres Kahele — [4]: "co-founded Nā Palapalai"
- Nā Palapalai — [4]: "co-founded Nā Palapalai"
- Dennis Pavao — [6]: "started the group Hui ʻ Ohana"
- Hui ʻ Ohana — [6]: "started the group Hui ʻ Ohana"
- Cold Case Files / Onyx — [7]: "Cold Case Files is a compilation album released by rap group, Onyx"

Ambiguities:
- Name variants for the same musician appear: "Israel Kamakawiwoʻole" (with ʻole glyph), "Israel Kamakawiwo'ole" (apostrophe), and "Israel \"Iz\" Kamakawiwo'ole".
- No passage names any specific Hawaiian group membership for Israel; multiple Hawaiian groups are mentioned elsewhere (e.g., "Nā Palapalai", "Hui ʻ Ohana") but not linked to Israel.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing fact: the name of the Hawaiian musical group that Israel Kamakawiwoʻole ("Braddah Iz") was a member of (group membership relation is not present in the passages).

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch the Israel Kamakawiwoʻole biography page: "Israel Kamakawiwoʻole" (Wikipedia article) or query "Israel Kamakawiwoʻole Wikipedia".
- Fetch the likely group page: "Makaha Sons of Niʻihau" (page) or query "Makaha Sons of Niihau Israel Kamakawiwoole".
- Query: "Israel Kamakawiwo'ole band membership" or "Which band was Braddah Iz a member of?"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.

The band started its career as an opening act at a small nightclub called Uptown Yokos.  The group has reorganized several times, with some members seeking other professional careers.  After the deaths of the Kamakawiwoʻole brothers Skippy and Israel, the remaining members dropped the "of Niihau" and became The Makaha Sons.

Wonderful World (Israel Kamakawiwo'ole album) | Wonderful World is an album by the Hawaiian musician Israel Kamakawiwo'ole released 2007, a decade after his death in 1997.  The album is considered a classic and suggested in some tourist guides as representative of Hawaiian contemporary music.
E Ala E | E Ala Ē is an album by the Hawaiian singer Israel Kamakawiwo'ole, released in 1995.
Ka 'Ano'i | Ka ʻ Anoʻ i is the debut album by Hawaiian singer Israel Kamakawiwo'ole.
N Dis Life | In Dis Life is the final album of original music by Native Hawaiian singer Israel "Iz" Kamakawiwo'ole before his death, released in 1996.  It reached #3 at Top World Music Albums.

Moe Keale | Wilfred Nalani "Moe" Keale (December 3, 1939 - April 15, 2002) was a musician of Hawaiian music, a ukulele virtuoso, and an American actor.  He was the uncle and major musical influence of Israel Kamakawiwo'ole.

response.summary_2: Israel Kamakawiwoʻole (also called "Braddah Iz") was a founding member of the Mākaha Sons of Ni'ihau.
